item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was certainly very cute and amusing, but the story is a bit lacking. The Interview log was absolute hilarious, enough so to get an upvote from that alone. I do think the story needs more, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I got to read "splashy time" I was already prepared to upvote, expecting a much needed light-hearted skip. However, all of this quickly went full 180 after I started reading the description. Using the designations and mentioning the recovery before actually saying what the objects are (in the 3rd paragraph no less) is a huge pet peeve of mine. This alone would warrant a downvote on any skip, no matter how brilliantly well-written it would otherwise be. Then there's the interview transcript, which I saw others enjoyed: it brings nothing new to the table (we already know the effects from the researcher-doctor text convo) and it makes sense that as soon as someone noticed the issue it be removed and rewritten properly.

I don't expect anyone to fix these issues since it's been so long since the article was written (which would normally rend this a necropost, for which I apologise), but if it happens I'll change my vote. Till then, however, it's a very firm downvote

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is to be assumed it is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could be used as encryption if it remembers what the peeps are supposed to mean

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: whoops! I realized just after I posted this that the separation of the birds was actually explained. my bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was so cute, and so fun to read. What a nice, lighthearted piece! Made me smile.

I still have questions about a few things after reading it, like: does 2243-C have the same anomalous effects on other birds, or just these two? Can 2243-A and -B display these properties when they are separated? There could have been lots of experiments to play around with this kind of thing. Also, What kind of conversations were had between researchers and the birds (foundation members could have gone outside the radius to report what was said). I would have loved to know what the birds were saying, since "splashy time" was so adorable.

I also thought how useful this kind of thing could be to linguists, and whether or not the "peeps" and "chirps" had any kind of meaningful grammatical structure…but that's a linguistics student's POV :P

item.title: Re: Request for testing
item.content:encoded:
WAY LATE, but I read through the linked scip and I'm not sure how they would fit. Unless you're going to have Genser grow an arm and write within reading distance of someone as 2243 has a limited effective range. The translation effect is mainly noticed with speech and fish can't talk so the option would be to have the communication done in writing, but then you would have to think about if you'd mention what language Genser thought it was writing which would perhaps give need to a more fleshed out back story.

It also sounds rather aggressive so sticking it in a room with a bird cage with two little birds then asking it to write while someone watches sound like a recipe for a loss of at least 1 SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't thought about that, but based how I set up the audio/visual surveillance effects I think you're on to something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why hasn't anyone pointed out that not only it is a universal translator, it is also an infallible anti-surveillance device? Any conversation within the effect radius is eavesdropping, tapping and tamper-proof.

If a thing with soundproofed walls can be devised, this can be used as a way to communicate with victims of speech-based cognitohazards, I think?

item.title: Request for testing
item.content:encoded:
I have my own scip in the sandbox as of right now, and I was looking for a suitable translator to help with a bit of "added mystery" that I'm trying to work in. So far this scip seems like the best option, but you didn't reply to my PM (and I'm not even sure if you saw it), so I'm going to ask once more here. If you don't want me to use your scip, just let me know, and I'll stop bothering you.
Cheers

Edit: If you want to check over my scip, I'll link it here
http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/genser

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted due to the various issues mentioned by others, but PM me if this gets a rewrite so I can read said rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*cries*
This

this is beautiful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry if this has already been mentioned in the comments or the article itself, but could researchers simply remember the conversations to the best of their ability and transcribe that afterward? It wouldn't be an entirely accurate conversation, but it would be worth more to the Foundation than the peeps and chirps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that is implied, but it could be more clearly stated. As it is it isn't clear if the specific birds are actually relevant or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Separately SCP-2243-A, SCP-2243-B, SCP-2243-C behave as one would expect objects and creatures they resemble to behave. When placed within 1.9 m of each other SCP-2243 becomes active.

It's apparently a gestalt anomaly- the three together activate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2 containers

Both MLA and API would have this as "two" and if there is an accepted English style guide that differs here, I have never seen it.

The precision of the measurements in the Containment procedures isn't needed, really any avian cage large enough for two small birds is adequate, as far as I can tell.

I think this is a great concept but the execution is half-baked enough that I am downvoting. Specifically:

  • "splashy time" Really? Is this written for toddlers? Why isn't this just "bathing"?
  • The accidental discovery by a Foundation employee.
  • If -C is the source of the anomaly, then why isn't there evidence showing this (such as removing the birds and replacing the birds)? If -C isn't the source of the anomaly, then why is it an SCP object?

I'd really like to use this in future works, in the ways that Scorpion451 suggests, but I'm reluctant to refer to this article in it's current sloppy state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For my 2 cents, this all comes down to "A cute idea is not sufficient for a good skip, but a sufficiently cute and novel idea goes a long way toward making a good skip"

Also, I think Test A demonstrates how to record the conversations with the birds (not to mention how to use it for useful universal translation). It requires an almost comically complex setup, however, based on the fact that you can remember the conversation as something other than peeps and cheeps:

  • You hold the interview as normal, with a piece of recording equipment inside of SCP-2243.
  • Afterward, someone with good auditory short term memory plays back the recording within the radius.
  • As needed, they pause the recording, step outside the radius, and transcribe/recite what they heard for recording equipment outside the radius.
  • Redundant transcriptions in parallel languages could increase semantic accuracy for human-to-human translations where ambiguity is a concern.

Thus creating what is simultaneously the most accurate and least efficient universal translator possible.

Doing the reverse would make a secure way to store ultra sensitive documentation, though you'd risk loosing access if anything happened to SCP-2243: a disk full of peeps and cheeps outside 2243 becomes a list of access codes to class 4 documentation inside of it, for instance. Not suggesting this last part necessarily be put into the article, because of said fragility, but still an interesting idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Several very valid criticisms that have been mentioned to some extent by a few others and some that haven't, so I'm going to work on addressing them! One off the bat is the 3 and 4 m, simple typo. Thank you for catching it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures were mentioned by several people so I've removed the black box related items. Thanks for the input!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'll swap it out, sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got similar feed back from several people so I removed/reworded the containment so as to remove the black boxes.

item.title: Re: :-)
item.content:encoded: Ah so should it be site director then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm fairly sure there are examples of living creatures that are classified as Safe- the effect is fairly simple, and there are unlikely to be future complications with containment, so I would say this one was Safe.

I agree with the black boxing criticism though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The success of this skip is intriguing, as it breaks a host of site taboos:

  • redaction in Containment Procedures
  • extremely lax containment of an anomaly just because it isn't obviously harmful
  • alternate terms for Clearance Levels
  • obvious conversion of imperial measurements to metric
  • accidental discovery of the anomaly
  • documents the inclusion of which makes no sense in-universe, including lolFoundation researcher comments
  • a test log that simply repeats information already given, rather than developing the story

But the reasons I downvoted were these:

  • the idea is described, but never developed. There are lots of directions this could be taken - omni-translation, the psychology of birds - but nothing is particularly explored. I was so disappointed when I found that we weren't getting to hear the birds as characters - that seems a huge missed opportunity.
  • the Foundation sends multiple researchers/containment specialists to CP's house to clean his birdcage twice a week - that is a massive waste of resources.
  • the Foundation has a universal translator but considers it better to send its negotiations (potentially with alien intelligences) to CP's house, rather than keep the birdcage at a secure location.
  • the blackboxing of names is pointless. The Foundation can't have many MTF operatives with the initials CP (and we even know the number of letters in each name) - so if I can read this redacted document, I can find out the redacted information.
  • you mention what happens within a 3.02m radius (why so exact?) and outside a 4.02m radius, but not what happens in between.
  • why does the researcher angrily suggest that future observations and transcriptions are to be made remotely? You've already told us that remote observations will come across as bird noises.

A cute idea is not sufficient for a good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a nice SCP. There's definitely some mystery in the lightheartedness of two birds that can communicate at the level of a small child with others. The chirped interview with its black boxes made me chuckle to imagine what they might still want to redact, even if it's chirps or peeping.

I do have to note, though, that I feel that the black boxes in the Containment Procedures are usually not a good idea… To anyone who would need to have this SCP right now, or finds a pair of 'talking finches' that is accidentally (or intentionally) misplaced on-site, who would they contact?

Also, I believe living creatures are usually classified as Euclid, as you can't just stuff them in a box and never look at them again.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Author, please don't use images larger than they have to be. In this case, you were using a 2300+ pixels wide image that was being resized to 300 pixels. That eats bandwidth on mobile devices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was entertaining enough for me to upvote. A lighthearted anomaly that doesn't crush my soul is a very needed breath of fresh air every now and then.

I will say that you do a lot of blackboxing here that is not really necessary. This doesn't seem like the kind of SCP object that would warrant such high levels of censorship. You'd be better off just including the names. Also, blackboxing in the containment procedures is not something you should do if it can be helped, since that is the most crucial information from an in-universe standpoint, since thats how the object remains secured, contained, and protected.

But hey, thats just my opinion.

item.title: Re: :-)
item.content:encoded: I think what you mean when you say O-4 is "site director": O5 is the council, and O-1 through O-13 are the council members- they deal with stuff like political treaties and deciding whether they need to expand the moon base again. Site directors are below them, and handle the local stuff, and then you have a whole hierarchy of staff below them for each site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It explains why: it only works when all three are together- if you take away -C the effect stops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So uh, I fail to see the signifcnce of SCP-2234-C. It appears to just be a toy for the birds and actually has no anamolous properties. So why bother including it? Unless there is something more to it which isn't conveyed in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for mentioning it, it's actually my favorite part of the SCP hahaha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! That was what I was aiming for:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is my first SCP! I'm really glad it's been well received; I read about several people's first ones tanking so it's a bit of a relief haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the input and catch! I agree not knowing what the designations are referring to could be frustrating so I swapped the purchase section with the individual introduction.(also corrected the and to as)

item.title: Re: :-)
item.content:encoded: I see where you're coming from. I think trying to explain it in SCP would be a bit much, so I just knocked off the O-3 section and left it at O-4 approval.

I liked it! The internal logic of including that interview may be iffy, but it was amusing, as was the text message, and they both illustrate the effects well.
Edit: Beautiful! +1 is now firm, rather than tentative.
Still… \/

.              .
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a lot more going for it than against. I like that the effect only happens when these three more or less random and otherwise normal things are brought together. I'm glad you went further than just "talking animals". And yeah, I was amused by the log entirely made of peeps and chirps.

One thing I would change is moving the bit about where they were purchased down lower. I really do hate not knowing what the designations are once the description starts making reference to them. Also, this is an error:

will perceive SCP-2243-A and SCP-2243-B and sounding

Good skip. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it! The "this is completely unacceptable" made me smile as much as the interview of peep peeps.

Is this your first scip? If so, good job! If not, good job regardless!

Only complaint is that the dimensions of the cage seem overly precise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very fun, lighthearted scip. I appreciate that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The blackboxed peeps and chirps in the interview log made me lose it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad! I wanted it to be a bit lighthearted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview entirely in peeps & chirps made me smile. :)

But now I wonder… if they introduced another bird, would it be affected as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where you guys are coming from. I'll add in some addition observations taken after the in initial interview so it can be seen the foundation realized the problem and corrected it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those birds are probably saying stuff like "you you give me pump-a-lum" or some junk. In a practical sense though I like the way this is laid out for the most part. The interviews make this seem more like a -J though. I can understand the want to make the trope more active, but realistically I feel like they'd be able to just remember their conversations and write them down later. Seems you've already got all that covered, so good on ya. Regardless, it is amusing to the point that I'll +1 it :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea was the first interview was done and submitted without the writer realizing that it looked that way. Maybe I should add additional logs with tests recorded after the fact/ from a distance reflecting the reviewer's recommendation?

(also I corrected atat, thanks!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is amusing.

There's a couple of grammatical errors in the piece (an "at at" at one point, for example) but nothing that can't be overcome. I have to admit, the greatest problem I have with it would be the Foundation's decision to keep a sheet of peep-chirp in the file, when it would make more sense to transcribe the conversation later (proven possible by "splashy time", "Daisy", and "Tissle"), outside the area of effect. That said, I'm still upvoting because it's cute and funny, and I'm still thinking about how you'd have problems understanding their capabilities - or the conversations you could have with others near the birds that couldn't be recorded.
